name: anandyandawang-plugins
description: Personal Claude Code plugin marketplace. One folder = one plugin.

plugins.name: grug
plugins.description: Talk like grug brain developer. Very clear, very very simple. Fewest words, simple words, short sentences. Brief, high-level — no overexplain. Keeps all technical truth + code exact. Universal: chat, commits, PRs, code comments, reviews.

plugins.name: grug-extremist
plugins.description: grug but EXTREME — applies ONLY to Claude's chat/personality output; commits, PRs, code, comments, reviews and logs stay normal. Hard cap: at most 5-10 words per turn; if the user asks grug to explain, at most 50 words. Brevity beats clarity — short wins even for security/danger/destructive content (grug still says the danger, in few words). Third person 'grug', simple words; code and values stay byte-exact. complexity very very bad. word very bad too.

plugins.name: terse
plugins.description: Hard global cap on output verbosity: at most ~20% of a normal response. Strict, no exceptions. Every medium — chat, commits, PRs, code comments, reviews, logs, docs. Lead with the answer, then stop. Code and technical values stay exact and complete.

plugins.name: codecat
plugins.description: An ASCII-art cat companion that lives in your coding session. It pads in on its own — greets you when a session starts, loafs and does zoomies between turns, celebrates your git commits and pushes, and curls up at session end. Pure art delivered via hooks; never changes how Claude talks. Frequency is tunable.

plugins.name: clear-pr
plugins.description: Write PR titles and descriptions in anandyandawang's house style. Title: a lowercase imperative summary, like a commit subject (no ticket prefix). Body: `## What` (one or two lines of high-level outcome, not a diff summary) then `## Why` (the context the diff can't show — trigger, constraint, decision, alternatives). The diff already shows what changed and how; the description carries the high-level what and the why. Link evidence instead of inlining, backtick every code identifier, be honest about tradeoffs, size to the change. Auto-applies when opening a PR; invoke /clear-pr to apply the format on demand.

plugins.name: context-rich-pr
plugins.description: After a PR is created, enrich its description with context a human reviewer wants but the diff can't show. Triggers when a JIRA key (e.g. EX-12345) has been mentioned anywhere in the session: once a PR exists, fetch the ticket via the Atlassian MCP, add a `## JIRA` section linking it, fold the ticket's high-level what/why into the body, and attach Datadog links (traces, logs, metrics, dashboards) where they aid the why. A content enricher, not a formatter — completely independent of clear-pr. Invoke /context-rich-pr to enrich the current PR on demand.

plugins.name: clear-comments
plugins.description: Write code comments in anandyandawang's house style. Comment sparingly — the code says what, a comment says why. Vocabulary is `// TODO:` (with the unblocking condition, and the issue-tracker key in parens when the project uses one) and `// Note:` (an intentional, non-obvious decision); no FIXME/HACK/XXX, no `/* */` block comments, no commented-out code, no restating the line below. Backtick every code identifier. Doc comments are a mini-spec for public interfaces and complex services; inline `//` annotates mapping tables with the source system's label; tests get scenario/section banners. Always-on via hooks — keeps comments sparse and code self-documenting as a persistent default, not just when invoked.
